item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty scary if you ask me. Kinda seems like an anomalous effect coming from the marketing, making the emotions companies try to associate with their products literal and supernatural. The unnerving feeling comes from that kind of attachment and familiarity with the object being forced on the person observing it. One moment they look at it and suddenly they have fond memories of it. And then it even spreads to other people just by hearing it described, like a memetic thing. Kinda reminds me of Cousin Johnny, making it even creepier. Yeah it's just memories so far, but what if the effect grows stronger with testing or somehow changes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it would be interesting to tie this SCP in with the concept of hoarding somewhere. Like, maybe anyone who comes in contact with it will start to find fond memories of more and more insignificant until they eventually become a hoarder. This would essentially allow the object to spread a sort of "hoarder virus" that I think would give it more of a reason to be contained. People get nostalgic over everything to the point where I don't think there's any chance of society really noticing that this is an anomalous object were to just be left out in the world. For that matter, how would the foundation have ever been notified of the existence of such an insignificant item? I was on the fence about this article before writing this, but given time to think on it, this kinda bugs me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like it would be interesting to note that people mention the box being full, and perhaps thinking how much better it was before it became empty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The "ex-husband" comment is a nice touch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is a detail not mentioned, and I think it has important implications.
Was the box objectively in the agent's wedding video or not?
Would a completely unexposed random viewer, with no personal memory of the event to be effected, see the box on the tape? If no, then this rewrites memories only just the way it seems. But if yes, then it is actually changing history.

Writing challenge: how could the inexplicable appearance of a box of tissue disrupt major historical events?
-6 oz too much weight throws off the thrust calculations and Apollo 11 breaks up on re-entry
-a cameraman has something to wipe his lens with and gets a nun-blurry shot of bigfoot in a UFO -Elvis doesn't have to stretch for the TP roll, so instead has a heart attack later that day in a public place where he gets immediate medical attention, survives, takes the near miss as a sign from god, starts a kung-fu-baptist fitness cult that becomes the dominant religion of the American south

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Simple and well-written. I like it. As I was reading throughout the beginning of the description I was wondering, "Wouldn't it be cool if this SCP appeared in pictures of subjects who'd claimed to recognize it?" I was pretty surprised that exact thought played out in the article.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one. Short, sweet, simple, and well written, as well as being non-volatile, which is always a nice thing to mix in with acid-spewing lizard person skeletons and disaster-inducing/predicting Rubik's Cubes.
Also makes me think of random moments with Scotties tissues, but that's probably just my brain being weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of the best short articles I've seen. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've always liked this one. There's a strange, elusive sense of logic to that last line.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really interesting concept of attaching a strong anomalous effect to a 100% harmless object.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find this SCP OK and also quite redundant with a hint of humor added to it. Pretty much a few steps off from becoming a joke SCP.
Dunno how long it's been here, but congrats. I love this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: EDIT: I can't read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uninspired and not very noteworthy IMO. I'm surprised there are so many upvotes for this one. Downvote here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Simple, short, not "overpowered", not over-extended, not over-detailed.
Still weird and, frankly, quite a bit creepy if you stop to think about it.

I like it, gets an easy upvote from me, and, personally, I hope it doesn't get expanded. It's fine as it is, and expanding it might actually detract from it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Delightfully absurd. Upvoted!

Minor nitpick: why was it stored at a Reliquiary? I've just checked in the rules to be sure and I'm pretty certain this does not qualify as a "relic of historical or religious significance". Not to mention the fact that in Foundation nomenclature, Reliquiary is a prefix rather than a standalone facility type (fixed).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't know if I'd say dramatically different, but different enough. This one is at least well written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think expanding this would definitely help set its individuality. Some experiments never hurt.
But I like it in its current state also.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some SCPs are scary. Some SCPs are ominous. Some SCPs are funny.

This one is, in the words of Samuel L. Jackson, "just damn weird".

Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am misunderstood here again. I just mentioned the time-loop articles as an example of conceptually similar articles that are different enough for me to upvote each, as opposed to cognitohazard articles that are similar to the point of interchangeability, and thus, in my opinion, not worth keeping in more than one copy.

Eh, that's not the first high-rated article that I don't like, and not even the worst of them. I'll just go and ignore the existence of this one without further ranting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This isn't a time loop. It doesn't alter anything about the past, from what I can tell. It's a cognitive hazard that forces you to remember it in situations where it wasn't actually present, with strong feelings of nostalgia attached, and all that's necessary for these effects to manifest are knowledge of its existence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ugh.

1733 and 176 are quite similar, but I upvoted both of them, because each have unique interesting ideas not present in the other. There are a couple more time-loop articles, and I think I upvoted all of them, because they all have something unique and novel.

This one doesn't. And I am not saying that it sucks, because it doesn't: it is well written, the concept is interesting, and in other circumstances I could've upvoted it. The problem is, it is not different enough. Where each of the time-loop articles adds its own spin on the original concept, this one reads like a Frankenstein monster composed from ideas that were done before it. I have always put the ideas on the first place when rating articles, before execution and everything else, and this one just doesn't have any new ones.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCPs don't have to be creepy, just anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, I'd be hard pressed to find anything that wasn't similar to something that's been done before, whether on this wiki or in popular media as a whole.

Avatar and Pocahontas are figuratively the same story, but people still saw Avatar in droves.

But yeah, other than the potential memory alteration they are both pretty different.

Considering this is changing pictures and memories of people that haven't been exposed to it, it seems possible that it's actually inserting itself into history (which another SCP does as well.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…
We have two SCPs that have the exact same effect?
How is this getting upvotes? *le confused*

They're dramatically different spins on a similar concept. Personally, I like both.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "It's good but I've read something that if you squint right it kinda looks similar. It sucks then"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Zachary have been done before, emotions to inanimate objects were done before, anomalous object class have been done before, although I can't see how that could be considered interesting. This is well written but doesn't have any new ideas. Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…

We have two SCPs that have the exact same effect?
How is this getting upvotes? *le confused*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-1500

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you don't mind me asking, who's Zachary Callahan? Don't really get this article, is it a reference of some kind?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's kind of Zachary Callahan with a Kleenex box, but the effect is goofy enough that I like it. Also, the idea of upgrading from anomalous to actual SCP is neat.

(Why do my posts always end up as the first on the second page? This is starting to get to me.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I must be missing something, because I can't see what's creepy or clever about this. Is there a reference I'm not getting?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. I like that it's a powerful effect attached to something as utterly useless and harmless as a box of tissues. It makes me think that either (A), someone created this effect on purpose and attaching it to a box of tissues was a dry run for something bigger, or (B), the universe is just really weird, both of which are interpretations I like.

And yes, there are other articles which have mentioned an "Anomalous" classification. My rewrite of SCP-472 cribbed that idea from Bright (I don't know if it was ever his idea, but I took it from SCP-834).

To me, the implication is that if it's extremely easy to contain and interesting enough to the Foundation to merit attention, it's classified as Safe and given a full SCP slot (and an associated research team), and if it's extremely easy to contain but is not remotely interesting, it's classified as Anomalous. Maybe the Foundation once categorized Anomalous items as full SCPs until they found enough stupid knicknacks that were 'simply too useless to merit further attention' and put all items categorized as Anomalous into one database, and freed up their SCP numbers for use by other items. (It goes without saying, of course, that anything Anomalous is going to pass the "Safe" locked box test.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They don't make tissues like that anymore!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is the box always empty when you need to blow your nose? I remember when it was full…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent work. This is extremely clever. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, I remember this article from when I was 12! Kept me up at night.

Good stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They thought it was something else. It got upgraded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno, I like it as it is now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i like it. expand it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, Drexlore. Yes! There is another one: SCP-834.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a set of markers somewhere that make tattoos what come to life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it! Also, I believe that this is the second SCP to say that it was upgraded from Anomalous to an actual object class. Although I could be wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aww, I remember that box. Good times, good times.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. Simple, strange, dodges a lot of the cliches like being randomly indestructible. Only minor quibble is that there's no need for the "Anomalous Safe" designation. I would just make it Safe.
